strategy: agentic chunking via Gemini (headline + summary + original text)

pages.text:
1

STRETCHING

3

➢ Lecture
Stretching

If you're a committed weight lifter, chances are you're either stretching a lot already or
you think you should be stretching more. Historically, stretching has been considered
one of those "does no wrong" activities, the value of which is so self-evident it does not
require evidence to support it.

But over the last few decades, scientists have figured out that stretching isn't quite the
panacea people thought it was, because it doesn't work as many people think.

How stretching makes you more flexible
Many people have the intuitive idea that stretching makes your muscles longer. When
you're doing a stretch, at some point you can't stretch any further, supposedly because
you've reached the muscle's maximum length. So you may think stretching the muscle
increase its length, allowing you to reach more range of motion in the future. This is not
the case. During most stretching movements, you do not reach the muscle's maximum
length unless you break some bones along the way. Instead, the limiting factor is the
nervous system that does not allow you to reach what it registers as potentially
dangerous muscle lengths.

Not only is muscle length not the limiting factor of your range of motion in most practical
scenarios, you also cannot increase it with regular stretching protocols. Muscle length
isn't even the correct terminology to begin with, as a muscle is fixed in position between
2 bones. If you literally increased the biceps's whole length, it would become loose and
incapable of contracting effectively. Instead, when most people refer to muscle length,
they are referring to muscle fascicle length. Remember: muscle fascicles are muscle
fibers bound together by a surrounding sheet of perimysium. Muscle fascicles can run
all the way from one tendon or fascial endpoint of the muscle to the other end. Logically,
headings: How stretching makes you more flexible

pages.text:
4

there is a clear limit on how much muscle fascicles can increase in length while
preserving contractile functionality, but increases in muscle fascicle length of up to 34%
have been found. The longer a muscle's fascicles, the greater their extensibility and the
longer their range of motion (ROM).

Most stretching protocols do not affect muscle fascicle length. While very high-intensity,
painful stretching has been found to increase muscle fascicle length, most stretching
protocols do not affect a muscle's architecture. For example, Longo et al. (2021) found
no effect of 12 weeks of static stretching 5x per week on the muscle length, thickness,
strength or fascicle angle of the calves, and the participants weren't even trained.

Most of the long-term improvement in flexibility after conventional stretching is neural in
nature, not structural. In particular, neural stretch tolerance increases. The more
permissive the nervous system, the greater the range of motion (ROM) it allows the
muscle-tendon structures to reach. There are several neural mechanisms, including an
increase in pain threshold and decrease in agonist reflex activation, that contribute to
the increased extensibility. Nerves themselves can also become less stiff. We'll use
neural stretch tolerance as a catch-all, umbrella term for all neural processes here.

Other non-neural mechanisms that contribute to increased tissue extensibility after
stretching, AKA flexibility or ROM, include a decrease in passive stiffness and a
temporary increase in viscoelasticity.

Stiffness refers to the degree to a which a body part resists deformation by a force. In
other words, stretching makes it easier to lengthen the musculotendinous complex: less
force is required to achieve a given stretch position. Stiffness is often intuitively
regarded as a 'bad thing', but this depends on the context. Stiffness can make
powerlifters stronger, for example, as it provides them with passive resistance against
the loaded barbell on their back in a deep squat position. A stiff spine may also prevent
injury, as it's less likely to flex excessively. For the sake of flexibility though, stiffness is

5

of course undesirable. If you want to be able to do a full split, stiff adductors and
hamstrings will make that more difficult, so stretching helps make these muscles and
their tendons become more compliant to being stretched out.

Stretching also temporarily increases a muscle's viscoelasticity. Simply put, the more
elastic a muscle, the more it will be stretched out by a given force, but muscles are not
just elastic but viscoelastic. Elastic materials resist stretch in a very straightforward
manner: the more you stretch them out, the more resistance they provide. When you
release the force on them, they immediately return to their resting length. Viscosity is a
more complex and less intuitive property. It makes a muscle's response to stretching
rate and time dependent, like a thick liquid. Concretely, if you keep a muscle in a
stretched position, it will gradually reduce the resistance it provides. Stretching makes
muscles more elastic for a certain period.

To summarize, the following is what normally happens when you stretch a muscle.
1. Viscoelasticity may increase after hard stretching, as in over two minutes, but this
is only temporary. Depending on the amount of stretching, viscoelasticity returns
to baseline within about 10 minutes after two minutes of stretching; or 20 minutes
after 4-8 minutes of stretching; or an hour after some seriously hardcore yoga.
2. Neural stretch tolerance increases. As this is neural learning, like memorizing
words, this is a more permanent adaptation. However, the increased stretch
tolerance is lost over time and can be reinforced by repetition, much like words
are gradually lost from memory and reinforced by repetition.
3. Over the long run, musculotendinous stiffness may decrease, reducing their
passive resistance to being lengthened.
4. Muscle and tendon length stay the same after conventional stretching workouts.
All you do with most stretching programs is teach the nervous system that it's
okay to relax the muscle a bit more when stretched. Most of the neural
adaptation is an increase in pain tolerance. Your nervous system effectively
registers that the 'potentially dangerous muscle length' is evidently not
headings: 1. Viscoelasticity may increase after hard stretching, as in over two minutes, but this
after 4-8 minutes of stretching; or an hour after some seriously hardcore yoga.
2. Neural stretch tolerance increases. As this is neural learning, like memorizing
are gradually lost from memory and reinforced by repetition.
3. Over the long run, musculotendinous stiffness may decrease, reducing their

pages.text:
6

dangerous, as you can be in that position without any injury occurring. So the
range of motion at which pain occurs is moved up a bit and the nervous system
allows the muscle to reach longer lengths.

Let's emphasize for effect: most stretching protocols do not increase a muscle's
fascicle length. Any increases in range of motion still present the day after the
stretching are primarily the result of neural adaptations.

Strength training with heavy eccentric muscle contractions over a large range of motion
does actually increase muscle fascicle length considerably. This stimulates muscle
hypertrophy via addition of sarcomeres in series. Muscle fascicles are arranged into
series of sarcomeres, as illustrated below, so more sarcomeres lead to longer muscle
fascicles.

Muscle fascicles are arranged into series of sarcomeres. Source
headings: Let’s emphasize for effect: most stretching protocols do not increase a muscle’s
fascicle length. Any increases in range of motion still present the day after the

pages.text:
7

So if you take a bodybuilder and a yoga practitioner and compare their hamstring
muscle lengths, the bodybuilder likely is the one with the longer muscles.

Multiple meta-analyses have found that strength training is generally just as effective as
stretching to increase joint range of motion (ROM) [2, 3]. This finding is consistent
across body parts and for both active and passive range of motion. If you think about it,
this makes perfect sense, because full ROM strength training is essentially
weighted, dynamic stretching.

A perfect illustration of the neural rather than morphological nature of stretching's effect
on your flexibility is that stretching your lower body increases your upper body's range
of motion and vice versa. Stretching increases your body's stretch tolerance
systematically. However, by far the greatest increase in stretch tolerance still occurs
locally and is highly specific to the exact position of your stretch.

It's worth noting that some high-intensity stretching protocols can in fact increase
muscle length, but it takes very high amounts of stretching force on the muscle fibers to
achieve this, often requiring special equipment to force a more extreme stretch than
people can manually achieve. This type of machine-overloaded stretching can also
induce muscle growth, so it's more comparable to strength training than your average
yoga session. Even then, it may only work on muscles that can reach passive
insufficiency, in particular the calves and the hamstrings. We will discuss this type of
hardcore stretching in more detail in the module on advanced training techniques.

The above knowledge, of course, has far reaching implications for the use of stretching
in flexibility training, warming up, and postural correction.
headings: this makes perfect sense, because full ROM strength training is essentially

pages.text:
8

Flexibility training
The key implication of the knowledge that stretching's effects are normally purely neural
is that flexibility training should be highly purposeful and specific. Unlike what a change
in muscle length would achieve, stretching increases range of motion very specifically in
the practiced stretch position with limited carry-over to flexibility in other positions. As
such, stretching doesn't generally increase your mobility - the ability to produce injury-
free movement - during functional tasks.

More generally, flexibility is not a whole-body trait. Flexibility does not consistently
correlate with any health biomarker or all-cause mortality. The correlations that do exist
are likely mediated by muscle strength, not flexibility.

Stretching mainly just makes you better at stretching and even that's not
guaranteed if you're already doing strength training. A study by Simao et al. (2021)
found that the addition of static stretching to strength training did not improve flexibility
any further at all, despite the flexibility training specifically including the sit-and-reach-
test that was used to quantify flexibility. Thus, it makes no sense to just stretch every
muscle for the sake of it. Your stretching should have a purpose, if you do it.

Concretely, stretching is only beneficial if you have a certain exercise or movement you
want to do with more ROM than you currently can. This is not often the case. If you ask
people that do a lot of stretching which movement they're trying to increase the ROM of,
you'll notice they often haven't thought about it. It's not that difficult for a remotely
healthy person to achieve full ROM during most strength training exercises.

By far the most problematic strength training movement to achieve full ROM in is the
barbell squat. However, the problem here is generally exercise technique or stability,
rather than muscle extensibility. Most lifters have no problem getting their tissues into
the required position: they can do so easily while lying on the floor, in a hack squat
machine or with split squats. While supporting a barbell, however, reaching parallel
headings: Stretching mainly just makes you better at stretching and even that’s not

pages.text:
9

depth with relatively neutral lumbopelvic alignment becomes more difficult. The problem
is thus logically lack of stability rather than flexibility. Of course, body structure plays a
role, but the vast majority of trainees can in Menno's experience squat to at least
parallel if their technique is sound. Many trainees have experienced that all the
stretching in the world does not improve their squat depth. You know what really helps
you squat deeper? Squatting. There is nothing more specific to teach the nervous
system how to squat than squatting itself.

Two scenarios where static stretching is in fact often beneficial is reducing your grip
width on back squats and learning how to front squat with an Olympic grip (illustrated
below). For these purposes, you should make the stretching mimic the desired position
as closely as possible, as per the specificity principle. This means you should get under
the bar and get your hands as far into the desired position as you can without significant
pain. Try to approximate the desired position and hold it. For the front squat, you may
need to start with a wide grip and only a few or even just one finger under the bar before
you can move in your grip and put more fingers under the bar. Low-intensity static
stretching like this - just uncomfortable, not painful - can be as effective as harder
stretching to begin with. It doesn't matter much how long you hold the stretch, as long
as the total stretching time is significant (dose-response with diminishing returns) [2].
You'll notice it when you can move in your grip a bit. For maximum flexibility, higher-
intensity stretching will provide greater benefits.

The Olympic front squat grip.

10

If you have no specific positions or movements you want to do with more ROM than you
currently you can, you probably don't need to do any stretching to get more flexible. It
would just make you better at stretching. Many strength trainees do just fine without any
kind of stretching whatsoever.

11

Stretching as a warm-up
During the warm-up of a strength trainee, flexibility training may not just be useless but
even harmful if it involves intensive static stretching. Static stretching essentially tells a
muscle to relax, whereas during strength training we want the muscle to be highly
active. Static stretching can reduce motoneuron excitability in addition to muscle and
tendon stiffness. Thereby, static stretching can reduce muscle force production,
activation, power output and consequently strength and total work output during
training. These negative effects on performance can reduce training adaptations. Just
50 seconds of static stretching when warming up has been found to significantly reduce
muscle growth during a strength training program: see the infographic below. Strength
development was not affected in this study and multiple others [e.g. 2, 3], but some
research did find static stretching during the warm-up or intra-workout before each set
decreased strength development. A 2022 meta-analysis found a weak trend for strength
development to be slightly to moderately impaired in strength training programs
combined with stretching programs compared to the training protocols without stretching
(p = 0.14). The negative effect of stretching became significantly greater with time and
became significant when including all literature regardless of quality. The reduction in
strength gains was almost entirely attributable to pre-workout stretching. The
researchers could not attribute the effect to a specific type of stretching, but it's likely
that only static stretching causes these effects, not dynamic stretching.

12

As such, static stretching is best avoided before your workouts, unless you have a very
good reason to do it, such as stretching to be able to front squat with an Olympic grip.
You don't have to be completely paranoid about static stretching pre-workout though if
you need to do it to, for example, get your wrists under the bar for front squats or stretch
your shoulders a bit to get a narrower grip for back squats. As long as you keep the
static stretching duration short and the intensity very low, it won't hurt your performance
or gains [2]. A good guideline is to keep the intensity below 50% of the point of
discomfort and the duration below 30 seconds. In other words, think 'gentle warm-up'
rather than 'pull to eviscerate'. Then again, for most people, there should also be no
reason to need static stretching before a workout in the first place.

Dynamic and ballistic stretching do not reduce muscle strength or growth, but they also
do not increase it. Many athletes feel strongly that dynamic stretching improves their
performance while objectively measuring performance shows this to be false.

13

See the course module on warming up for more information on how to warm up
optimally.

14

Stretching to prevent injury
Some stretching proponents argue that while you may not need to do stretching to
warm up or get more flexible, it's still very useful to prevent injuries. The idea is that
injuries occur when your tissues are stretched to near-maximum lengths, where they're
more susceptible to tearing. However, as you learned, most tissues do not remotely
approach maximum length during any kind of functional movement or strength training
exercise in the first place, and even if they do, stretching is unsuccessful to increase
tissue extensibility. Unsurprisingly then, flexibility is a poor predictor of injury risk. In fact,
hypermobility is in several studies related to increased injury risk, as you can end up in
positions your body is not built for. Your flexibility, as determined by your nervous
system's stretch tolerance is better seen as a protective mechanism than a structural
limitation.

Moreover, it is very difficult to predict how someone will perform complex movements by
performing tests of their flexibility and mobility. Functional movement screens are bad at
predicting how likely someone is to get injured. For example, a world class Olympic
weightlifter may have terrific exercise technique yet still score poorly on the arbitrary
stretching drills in a functional movement screen.

15

Since being more flexible does not protect you against injury, it should come as no
surprise that stretching does not protect you from injury [2, 3].

Any benefits of stretching for injury protection are generally temperature related, so a
simple general warm-up and some exercise specific warm-up sets make stretching
redundant for strength trainees.

In contrast, strength training is well documented to be protective against injury because
it directly makes your connective tissues stronger and your muscles more capable of
controlling your movements.

16

Stretching as active recovery
You may wonder if stretching still isn't a good form of active recovery. It's not. Static
stretching does not enhance muscle recovery after exercise. The idea that post-workout
stretching is a good idea was largely based on the idea that stretching reduces muscle
damage and soreness. While stretching can acutely reduce pain - that's the main thing
that stretching does, increasing your pain tolerance - stretching does nothing to
alleviate subsequent muscle damage or soreness [2].

There are in fact good reasons why stretching is a poor form of active recovery.
• Stretching can reduce IGF-1 production during strength training.
• In contrast to popular belief, stretching reduces the flow of blood and oxygen to
your muscles and tendons [2]. The effect is comparable to the effect you achieve
with a blood flow restriction cuff for KAATSU training. The effect is reduced in
more flexible individuals, including women, but it's generally still negative.
Afterwards there is a rebound effect, but for the promotion of blood flow you're
often probably better off doing low intensity exercise rather than stretching.
• There is a trend in research that combining stretching, even dynamic stretching,
with strength training decreases strength development.

So, if you want to spend more time being active, first consider increasing your training
frequency, which may actually be beneficial in speeding up your rate of recovery (see
the course topic on training frequency). Low intensity physical activity, such as walking,
may also be beneficial by improving blood flow (see course module on injury
management).

17

Flexibility for performance
Many people are afraid to go without any stretching because of the stigma of
bodybuilders as clumsy, 'muscle bound' (whatever that means) monsters. It's easy to
imagine a bodybuilder tearing off a doorknob or accidentally crushing a glass in his
hand because he "doesn't know his own strength." Yet it's a complete myth that
bodybuilding makes you clumsy. Research from Smits-Engelsman et al. (2008) and
unpublished research from Panjan et al. in Slovenia show that the ability to control your
force has no relation with how strong you are.

In fact, if anything you'd theoretically expect improved motor control in bodybuilders due
to the positive neural adaptations that take place as you gain strength. Bodybuilders are
also renowned for their mind-muscle connection. While we don't have much research
on this, bodybuilders have been shown to be able to control the contraction of opposing
muscle groups at the same time better than untrained individuals.

Perhaps the most convincing research is that in dancers. Kudrna et al. from DeSales
University have looked at the effect of strength training in female artistic dancers. The
strength training did not impair their flexibility, posture or dance specific abilities, such
as the height of the active and passive developé poses, but it did improve self-rated
strength, posture and confidence during dancing.

18

The full split became relatively popular in bodybuilding posing routines at some point,
presumably because starkly contrasted with the stigma of bodybuilders as 'muscle
bound'.

More generally, flexibility does not consistently correlate with sports performance in
competitive athletes or functional daily living ability in the elderly, whereas strength
training very significantly improves both.

It's worth noting that untrained individuals can gain a little bit of strength from stretching.
A 2022 meta-analysis found that compared to doing exercise at all, stretching resulted
in significant albeit minor strength development. Strength improved only during dynamic
movements, not isometrically, indicating the strength was likely not the result of muscle
hypertrophy or an increase in muscle activation. Stretching can potentiate the ability of
tendons to store energy when they are lengthened, resulting in greater passive force
production (better elasticity, essentially). As we'll discuss in the module on advanced
training techniques, some forms of static stretching can in fact induce muscle
hypertrophy, but the stretching protocols have to be quite hardcore for that.

19

Take-home messages
• Stretching doesn't normally make your muscles longer. It mainly increases your
neural stretch tolerance, in particular your pain threshold during the stretch. This
makes your nervous system allow you a higher range of motion. Any flexibility
improvements that persist more than about 30 minutes are generally mostly
neural in nature.
• Stretching is practically only beneficial to increase the ROM of a movement or
position you currently don't have full ROM in. In this scenario, the best stretching
drill is the most specific one: the best way to improve your squat depth is thus to
squat more and improve your squat technique, not to perform a lot of non-
specific stretching drills.
• You don't need to do any stretching as part of your warm-up, although dynamic
stretching drills are a fine way to warm up. Static stretching pre-workout can even
be detrimental for performance and progress.
• Stretching does not protect you against injury.
• Stretching does not aid post-exercise recovery. It may even be detrimental. More
dynamic physical activity is preferred as active recovery.
• Most strength trainees do not need to do any kind of stretching.

20

Learning objectives
After having studied this module, you should be able to do following.
• Explain how stretching can increase joint range of motion.
• Explain why weight training can be more effective than stretching to increase
muscle fascicle length.
• Explain why stretching doesn't do much to increase your squat depth.
• Explain why a functional movement screen does not predict injury risk well for
movements not in the test.
• Explain why static stretching pre-workout can hinder strength training
adaptations.
• Explain why static stretching is not a good recovery enhancement technique.

guaranteed if you're already doing strength training. A study by Simao et al. (2021)
found that the addition of static stretching to strength training did not improve flexibility
any further at all, despite the flexibility training specifically including the sit-and-reach-
test that was used to quantify flexibility. Thus, it makes no sense to just stretch every
muscle for the sake of it. Your stretching should have a purpose, if you do it.

Concretely, stretching is only beneficial if you have a certain exercise or movement you
want to do with more ROM than you currently can. This is not often the case. If you ask
people that do a lot of stretching which movement they're trying to increase the ROM of,
you'll notice they often haven't thought about it. It's not that difficult for a remotely
healthy person to achieve full ROM during most strength training exercises.

By far the most problematic strength training movement to achieve full ROM in is the
barbell squat. However, the problem here is generally exercise technique or stability,
rather than muscle extensibility. Most lifters have no problem getting their tissues into
the required position: they can do so easily while lying on the floor, in a hack squat
machine or with split squats. While supporting a barbell, however, reaching parallel


--- Page 9 ---
9

depth with relatively neutral lumbopelvic alignment becomes more difficult. The problem
is thus logically lack of stability rather than flexibility. Of course, body structure plays a
role, but the vast majority of trainees can in Menno's experience squat to at least
parallel if their technique is sound. Many trainees have experienced that all the
stretching in the world does not improve their squat depth. You know what really helps
you squat deeper? Squatting. There is nothing more specific to teach the nervous
system how to squat than squatting itself.

Two scenarios where static stretching is in fact often beneficial is reducing your grip
width on back squats and learning how to front squat with an Olympic grip (illustrated
below). For these purposes, you should make the stretching mimic the desired position
as closely as possible, as per the specificity principle. This means you should get under
the bar and get your hands as far into the desired position as you can without significant
pain. Try to approximate the desired position and hold it. For the front squat, you may
need to start with a wide grip and only a few or even just one finger under the bar before
you can move in your grip and put more fingers under the bar. Low-intensity static
stretching like this - just uncomfortable, not painful - can be as effective as harder
stretching to begin with. It doesn't matter much how long you hold the stretch, as long
as the total stretching time is significant (dose-response with diminishing returns) [2].
You'll notice it when you can move in your grip a bit. For maximum flexibility, higher-
intensity stretching will provide greater benefits.

The Olympic front squat grip.


--- Page 10 ---
10

If you have no specific positions or movements you want to do with more ROM than you
currently you can, you probably don't need to do any stretching to get more flexible. It
would just make you better at stretching. Many strength trainees do just fine without any
kind of stretching whatsoever.


--- Page 11 ---
11

Stretching as a warm-up
During the warm-up of a strength trainee, flexibility training may not just be useless but
even harmful if it involves intensive static stretching. Static stretching essentially tells a
muscle to relax, whereas during strength training we want the muscle to be highly
active. Static stretching can reduce motoneuron excitability in addition to muscle and
tendon stiffness. Thereby, static stretching can reduce muscle force production,
activation, power output and consequently strength and total work output during
training. These negative effects on performance can reduce training adaptations. Just
50 seconds of static stretching when warming up has been found to significantly reduce
muscle growth during a strength training program: see the infographic below. Strength
development was not affected in this study and multiple others [e.g. 2, 3], but some
research did find static stretching during the warm-up or intra-workout before each set
decreased strength development. A 2022 meta-analysis found a weak trend for strength
development to be slightly to moderately impaired in strength training programs
combined with stretching programs compared to the training protocols without stretching
(p = 0.14). The negative effect of stretching became significantly greater with time and
became significant when including all literature regardless of quality. The reduction in
strength gains was almost entirely attributable to pre-workout stretching. The
researchers could not attribute the effect to a specific type of stretching, but it's likely
that only static stretching causes these effects, not dynamic stretching.


--- Page 12 ---
12

As such, static stretching is best avoided before your workouts, unless you have a very
good reason to do it, such as stretching to be able to front squat with an Olympic grip.
You don't have to be completely paranoid about static stretching pre-workout though if
you need to do it to, for example, get your wrists under the bar for front squats or stretch
your shoulders a bit to get a narrower grip for back squats. As long as you keep the
static stretching duration short and the intensity very low, it won't hurt your performance
or gains [2]. A good guideline is to keep the intensity below 50% of the point of
discomfort and the duration below 30 seconds. In other words, think 'gentle warm-up'
rather than 'pull to eviscerate'. Then again, for most people, there should also be no
reason to need static stretching before a workout in the first place.

Dynamic and ballistic stretching do not reduce muscle strength or growth, but they also
do not increase it. Many athletes feel strongly that dynamic stretching improves their
performance while objectively measuring performance shows this to be false.


--- Page 13 ---
13

See the course module on warming up for more information on how to warm up
optimally.


--- Page 14 ---
14

Stretching to prevent injury
Some stretching proponents argue that while you may not need to do stretching to
warm up or get more flexible, it's still very useful to prevent injuries. The idea is that
injuries occur when your tissues are stretched to near-maximum lengths, where they're
more susceptible to tearing. However, as you learned, most tissues do not remotely
approach maximum length during any kind of functional movement or strength training
exercise in the first place, and even if they do, stretching is unsuccessful to increase
tissue extensibility. Unsurprisingly then, flexibility is a poor predictor of injury risk. In fact,
hypermobility is in several studies related to increased injury risk, as you can end up in
positions your body is not built for. Your flexibility, as determined by your nervous
system's stretch tolerance is better seen as a protective mechanism than a structural
limitation.

Moreover, it is very difficult to predict how someone will perform complex movements by
performing tests of their flexibility and mobility. Functional movement screens are bad at
predicting how likely someone is to get injured. For example, a world class Olympic
weightlifter may have terrific exercise technique yet still score poorly on the arbitrary
stretching drills in a functional movement screen.


--- Page 15 ---
15

Since being more flexible does not protect you against injury, it should come as no
surprise that stretching does not protect you from injury [2, 3].

Any benefits of stretching for injury protection are generally temperature related, so a
simple general warm-up and some exercise specific warm-up sets make stretching
redundant for strength trainees.

In contrast, strength training is well documented to be protective against injury because
it directly makes your connective tissues stronger and your muscles more capable of
controlling your movements.


--- Page 16 ---
16

Stretching as active recovery
You may wonder if stretching still isn't a good form of active recovery. It's not. Static
stretching does not enhance muscle recovery after exercise. The idea that post-workout
stretching is a good idea was largely based on the idea that stretching reduces muscle
damage and soreness. While stretching can acutely reduce pain - that's the main thing
that stretching does, increasing your pain tolerance - stretching does nothing to
alleviate subsequent muscle damage or soreness [2].

There are in fact good reasons why stretching is a poor form of active recovery.
• Stretching can reduce IGF-1 production during strength training.
• In contrast to popular belief, stretching reduces the flow of blood and oxygen to
your muscles and tendons [2]. The effect is comparable to the effect you achieve
with a blood flow restriction cuff for KAATSU training. The effect is reduced in
more flexible individuals, including women, but it's generally still negative.
Afterwards there is a rebound effect, but for the promotion of blood flow you're
often probably better off doing low intensity exercise rather than stretching.
• There is a trend in research that combining stretching, even dynamic stretching,
with strength training decreases strength development.

So, if you want to spend more time being active, first consider increasing your training
frequency, which may actually be beneficial in speeding up your rate of recovery (see
the course topic on training frequency). Low intensity physical activity, such as walking,
may also be beneficial by improving blood flow (see course module on injury
management).


--- Page 17 ---
17

Flexibility for performance
Many people are afraid to go without any stretching because of the stigma of
bodybuilders as clumsy, 'muscle bound' (whatever that means) monsters. It's easy to
imagine a bodybuilder tearing off a doorknob or accidentally crushing a glass in his
hand because he "doesn't know his own strength." Yet it's a complete myth that
bodybuilding makes you clumsy. Research from Smits-Engelsman et al. (2008) and
unpublished research from Panjan et al. in Slovenia show that the ability to control your
force has no relation with how strong you are.

In fact, if anything you'd theoretically expect improved motor control in bodybuilders due
to the positive neural adaptations that take place as you gain strength. Bodybuilders are
also renowned for their mind-muscle connection. While we don't have much research
on this, bodybuilders have been shown to be able to control the contraction of opposing
muscle groups at the same time better than untrained individuals.

Perhaps the most convincing research is that in dancers. Kudrna et al. from DeSales
University have looked at the effect of strength training in female artistic dancers. The
strength training did not impair their flexibility, posture or dance specific abilities, such
as the height of the active and passive developé poses, but it did improve self-rated
strength, posture and confidence during dancing.


--- Page 18 ---
18

The full split became relatively popular in bodybuilding posing routines at some point,
presumably because starkly contrasted with the stigma of bodybuilders as 'muscle
bound'.

More generally, flexibility does not consistently correlate with sports performance in
competitive athletes or functional daily living ability in the elderly, whereas strength
training very significantly improves both.

It's worth noting that untrained individuals can gain a little bit of strength from stretching.
A 2022 meta-analysis found that compared to doing exercise at all, stretching resulted
in significant albeit minor strength development. Strength improved only during dynamic
movements, not isometrically, indicating the strength was likely not the result of muscle
hypertrophy or an increase in muscle activation. Stretching can potentiate the ability of
tendons to store energy when they are lengthened, resulting in greater passive force
production (better elasticity, essentially). As we'll discuss in the module on advanced
training techniques, some forms of static stretching can in fact induce muscle
hypertrophy, but the stretching protocols have to be quite hardcore for that.


--- Page 19 ---
19

Take-home messages
• Stretching doesn't normally make your muscles longer. It mainly increases your
neural stretch tolerance, in particular your pain threshold during the stretch. This
makes your nervous system allow you a higher range of motion. Any flexibility
improvements that persist more than about 30 minutes are generally mostly
neural in nature.
• Stretching is practically only beneficial to increase the ROM of a movement or
position you currently don't have full ROM in. In this scenario, the best stretching
drill is the most specific one: the best way to improve your squat depth is thus to
squat more and improve your squat technique, not to perform a lot of non-
specific stretching drills.
• You don't need to do any stretching as part of your warm-up, although dynamic
stretching drills are a fine way to warm up. Static stretching pre-workout can even
be detrimental for performance and progress.
• Stretching does not protect you against injury.
• Stretching does not aid post-exercise recovery. It may even be detrimental. More
dynamic physical activity is preferred as active recovery.
• Most strength trainees do not need to do any kind of stretching.


--- Page 20 ---
20

Learning objectives
After having studied this module, you should be able to do following.
• Explain how stretching can increase joint range of motion.
• Explain why weight training can be more effective than stretching to increase
muscle fascicle length.
• Explain why stretching doesn't do much to increase your squat depth.
• Explain why a functional movement screen does not predict injury risk well for
movements not in the test.
• Explain why static stretching pre-workout can hinder strength training
adaptations.
• Explain why static stretching is not a good recovery enhancement technique.
